summary: DeVault uses an in-flight entertainment system that forces ads on passengers as a springboard to discuss 'unlikable' software — features deliberately designed against the user's interests. He argues that overriding a passenger's brightness preference to show ads is a clear example of anti-features, where engineers knowingly worsen user experience for corporate benefit. He connects this to the broader pattern of dark patterns across the software industry. He concludes with a simple ethical stance: programmers should respect users and refuse to build features that subvert their interests.
key_points: Software that overrides user preferences to serve corporate interests is fundamentally disrespectful
The same technical mechanism used for legitimate safety announcements gets repurposed for advertising — a bait-and-switch of user trust
Dark patterns and anti-features are pervasive across the industry, not isolated incidents
Programmers bear personal responsibility for the features they build and should push back against demands to harm users
The ethical principle is simple: just be nice to people
money_quotes: A software engineer sat down and deliberately wrote a "feature" (or anti-feature?) which they had to have known that the user would not have wanted.
The airplane manufacturer demanded it at the expense of the user.
Many programmers have implemented something which measurably worsens the experience for the user in order to obtain some perceived benefit for the company they work for.
I find this behavior to be incredibly disrespectful to the user.

I do respect the user, I find satisfaction in making software which makes their lives better, and I always have and always will push back against anyone who demands that I subvert that ethos for their wallet's sake.
You should always aim to make the user's experience more pleasant, not more unpleasant.
We should just be nice to people. That's it: please be nice to people.
key_insight: Programmers have a moral responsibility to refuse building features that deliberately worsen the user's experience for corporate gain.
